title: Variables - giving things names
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: A variable is a name bound to a value. Assignment with = creates or rebinds the name.
explanation: When you write city = 'Seoul', Python does not put the value in a box - it ties a name to it, like a label on a string. Names can be retied to new values at any time, and the old value quietly lets go.
code_example:
city = "Seoul"
year = 2026
city = "Busan"   # retied
print(city, year)

exercises.question: What does this print?
hints: Read the lines top to bottom - what happens to 'city' on line 3?
Assignment does not keep history. A name only remembers its latest value.
By the time print() runs, 'city' was last assigned "Busan".

exercises.question:
And this one?

x = 5
y = x
x = 9
print(y)
hints: y was tied to the value of x at that moment, not to x itself.
When x is retied to 9, y doesn't follow - it kept the value it was given.
y was set to 5 and nothing touched it again.

exercises.question:
Which single symbol binds a name to a value?

mood _ "okay"
hints: It's one character, between the name and the value.
Not == (that asks a question) - the one that assigns.
